Exploring NASA Potentially Hazardous Asteroid Classifications

Ryan Horsey, Scott Engstrom, Pablo Leguizamo, Shashank Koneru

2026-05-04

Introduction

Near-Earth Objects (NEOs) are asteroids and comets whose orbits bring them near Earth’s orbit. NASA classifies some near-Earth asteroids as Potentially Hazardous Asteroids (PHAs), meaning their size and orbital path make them worth continued monitoring. The label sounds alarming, but it does not mean an asteroid is expected to hit Earth. It means the object is large enough and close enough to deserve attention.

This project explores which physical, orbital, and close-approach measurements separate asteroids NASA labels as hazardous from those it does not. We use data wrangling and visualization as the main tools, then use one classification tree as a simple check on which variables are most useful for reproducing NASA’s label.

A key skepticism point is that NASA’s PHA definition is partly based on absolute magnitude, which is related to asteroid size. We therefore compare a full feature set to a skeptical feature set that removes size and brightness. This gives the report a clear roadmap: describe the data, explore the main variable patterns, test whether the same patterns appear in a simple model, and explain the limits of the findings.

Data

The primary dataset is neo_full_dataset.xlsx, a local object-level dataset with one row per asteroid. We use this file instead of neo.csv because neo.csv is event-like: it contains repeated asteroid IDs from multiple close approaches. Using repeated object rows without grouped splitting could leak the same asteroid into both training and test sets.

library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(rpart)
library(knitr)
data_path <- "neo_full_dataset.xlsx"
if (!file.exists(data_path)) {
  data_path <- "ds-2020-final/neo_full_dataset.xlsx"
}

neo_raw <- read_excel(data_path)

tibble(
  rows = nrow(neo_raw),
  columns = ncol(neo_raw)
) %>%
  kable(caption = "Raw dataset dimensions")
Raw dataset dimensions
rows columns
42919 11

Data Dictionary

Data dictionary for variables used in the report
variable source unit role notes
neo_reference_id NASA NeoWs/SBDB extraction none Identifier Used only for duplicate checks, not modeling.
name NASA NeoWs/SBDB extraction none Identifier Object name; not used for modeling.
absolute_magnitude_h NASA NeoWs/SBDB extraction magnitude Predictor Brightness measure related to asteroid size; definition-adjacent.
est_diam_min_km NASA NeoWs/SBDB extraction kilometers Raw input Lower estimated diameter bound; replaced by average diameter.
est_diam_max_km NASA NeoWs/SBDB extraction kilometers Raw input Upper estimated diameter bound; replaced by average diameter.
diam_avg_km Engineered kilometers Predictor Mean of minimum and maximum estimated diameter.
miss_distance_km NASA NeoWs/SBDB extraction kilometers Predictor Close-approach miss distance; related to closeness but not the same as MOID.
relative_velocity_km_s NASA NeoWs/SBDB extraction km/s Predictor Relative velocity during close approach.
orbital_eccentricity NASA SBDB extraction none Predictor Orbital shape.
orbital_inclination NASA SBDB extraction degrees Predictor Tilt of orbit relative to reference plane.
orbital_semi_major_axis NASA SBDB extraction AU Predictor Average orbital distance scale.
is_potentially_hazardous_asteroid NASA NeoWs/SBDB extraction logical Target Converted to the factor variable hazardous.

est_diam_max_km 13 0.0%

orbital_inclination 4 0.0%
orbital_semi_major_axis 4 0.0%

Duplicate check for the object-level dataset
rows unique_neo_reference_ids duplicate_neo_reference_ids
42919 42919 0
Why the event-like CSV was not used as the main modeling table
dataset rows unique_ids interpretation
neo.csv 90836 27423 Event-like table with repeated asteroid IDs

Cleaning and Feature Engineering

model_vars_full <- c(
  "absolute_magnitude_h",
  "diam_avg_km",
  "miss_distance_km",
  "relative_velocity_km_s",
  "orbital_eccentricity",
  "orbital_inclination",
  "orbital_semi_major_axis"
)

model_vars_skeptical <- c(
  "miss_distance_km",
  "relative_velocity_km_s",
  "orbital_eccentricity",
  "orbital_inclination",
  "orbital_semi_major_axis"
)

neo_clean <- neo_raw %>%
  mutate(
    diam_avg_km = (est_diam_min_km + est_diam_max_km) / 2,
    hazardous = factor(
      is_potentially_hazardous_asteroid,
      levels = c(FALSE, TRUE),
      labels = c("Not Hazardous", "Hazardous")
    )
  ) %>%
  select(
    neo_reference_id,
    hazardous,
    all_of(model_vars_full)
  ) %>%
  drop_na(all_of(model_vars_full), hazardous)

tibble(
  raw_rows = nrow(neo_raw),
  cleaned_rows = nrow(neo_clean),
  rows_removed = nrow(neo_raw) - nrow(neo_clean),
  duplicate_ids_after_cleaning = sum(duplicated(neo_clean$neo_reference_id))
) %>%
  mutate(
    rows_removed_pct = format_percent(rows_removed / raw_rows)
  ) %>%
  select(raw_rows, cleaned_rows, rows_removed, rows_removed_pct, duplicate_ids_after_cleaning) %>%
  kable(caption = "Rows retained after cleaning and feature engineering")
Rows retained after cleaning and feature engineering
raw_rows cleaned_rows rows_removed rows_removed_pct duplicate_ids_after_cleaning
42919 41157 1762 4.1% 0

Exploratory Analysis

Class Balance

Class balance in the cleaned asteroid dataset
hazardous n prop
Not Hazardous 38434 93.4%
Hazardous 2723 6.6%

The hazardous class is much smaller than the non-hazardous class, so accuracy alone is not a reliable evaluation metric. A model can look accurate while missing many hazardous objects.

Feature Distributions by Hazard Class

The clearest separation appears in size-related variables: hazardous asteroids tend to have lower absolute magnitude values and larger estimated diameters. The orbital and close-approach variables show more overlap, which suggests that NASA’s label is not explained by one simple measurement in this dataset.

Relative Velocity vs. Miss Distance

This scatter plot shows why the problem is not as simple as “closer asteroids are always hazardous.” Hazardous and non-hazardous asteroids overlap across much of the velocity and miss-distance range.

Correlation Heatmap

Average diameter and absolute magnitude are strongly related, which matches the science: brighter asteroids are usually larger. This correlation is also part of why we avoid treating size and brightness as independent evidence in the skeptical model below.

Analysis Decisions and Trials

During earlier drafts, we tried a more model-heavy version of the analysis with logistic regression, classification trees, ROC curves, and precision-recall curves. Those tools did not change the main finding: size and brightness carry much of the signal, while the remaining orbital and close-approach variables are weaker by themselves. We therefore kept the final report focused on visual exploration and one interpretable classification tree. We also chose the object-level Excel file over the event-like CSV because repeated asteroid IDs in the CSV could make model evaluation look better than it really is.

Modeling

We use a classification tree because it is easier to explain than a more mathematical model: the tree sorts asteroids by splitting on variables that best separate hazardous from non-hazardous labels. The tree is fit twice:

  • Full feature set: orbital, close-approach, size, and brightness predictors.
  • Skeptical feature set: orbital and close-approach predictors only.

The skeptical feature set tests whether the model can still reproduce NASA’s label when we remove the variables most closely connected to the official PHA definition.

set.seed(2026)

neo_model <- neo_clean %>%
  mutate(row_id = row_number())

train_ids <- neo_model %>%
  group_by(hazardous) %>%
  slice_sample(prop = 0.80) %>%
  ungroup() %>%
  pull(row_id)

train_data <- neo_model %>%
  filter(row_id %in% train_ids) %>%
  select(-row_id, -neo_reference_id)

test_data <- neo_model %>%
  filter(!row_id %in% train_ids) %>%
  select(-row_id, -neo_reference_id)

tibble(
  split = c("Train", "Test"),
  rows = c(nrow(train_data), nrow(test_data)),
  hazardous_rate = c(
    mean(train_data$hazardous == "Hazardous"),
    mean(test_data$hazardous == "Hazardous")
  )
) %>%
  mutate(hazardous_rate = format_percent(hazardous_rate)) %>%
  kable(caption = "Stratified train/test split")
Stratified train/test split
split rows hazardous_rate
Train 32925 6.6%
Test 8232 6.6%

Evaluation Functions

We use four confusion-matrix metrics. Accuracy is the percent of all predictions that are correct. Recall is the percent of truly hazardous asteroids the model catches. Specificity is the percent of non-hazardous asteroids the model correctly leaves alone. Precision is the percent of predicted hazardous asteroids that are actually hazardous.

safe_divide <- function(numerator, denominator) {
  ifelse(denominator == 0, NA_real_, numerator / denominator)
}

confusion_metrics <- function(actual, predicted, positive = "Hazardous") {
  actual <- as.character(actual)
  predicted <- as.character(predicted)
  negative <- setdiff(unique(actual), positive)[1]

  tp <- sum(actual == positive & predicted == positive)
  tn <- sum(actual == negative & predicted == negative)
  fp <- sum(actual == negative & predicted == positive)
  fn <- sum(actual == positive & predicted == negative)

  tibble(
    tp = tp,
    tn = tn,
    fp = fp,
    fn = fn,
    accuracy = safe_divide(tp + tn, tp + tn + fp + fn),
    recall = safe_divide(tp, tp + fn),
    specificity = safe_divide(tn, tn + fp),
    precision = safe_divide(tp, tp + fp)
  )
}

Fit Classification Trees

full_formula <- as.formula(
  paste("hazardous ~", paste(model_vars_full, collapse = " + "))
)

skeptical_formula <- as.formula(
  paste("hazardous ~", paste(model_vars_skeptical, collapse = " + "))
)

tree_full <- rpart(
  full_formula,
  data = train_data,
  method = "class",
  control = rpart.control(cp = 0.001, minbucket = 25)
)

tree_skeptical <- rpart(
  skeptical_formula,
  data = train_data,
  method = "class",
  control = rpart.control(cp = 0.001, minbucket = 25)
)

tree_full_predicted <- predict(tree_full, newdata = test_data, type = "class")
tree_skeptical_predicted <- predict(tree_skeptical, newdata = test_data, type = "class")

Results

Tree Comparison

Classification tree performance on the test data
Feature Set Accuracy Recall Specificity Precision True Positives True Negatives False Positives False Negatives Note
Full 94.2% 32.5% 98.6% 61.7% 177 7577 110 368 Highest recall
Skeptical 93.4% 0.0% 100.0% NA 0 7687 0 545

The full tree performs better at identifying hazardous asteroids. The skeptical tree has similar overall accuracy, but it catches none of the hazardous objects in the test set. This is an important result: without size and brightness, the tree mostly learns to predict the majority “Not Hazardous” class.

Confusion Matrix Heatmap

The heatmap below uses the full classification tree, which has the best recall for hazardous asteroids.

The full tree correctly identifies some hazardous asteroids, but it still misses many of them. This keeps the result honest: the model helps show which variables matter, but it would not be acceptable as a real-world asteroid warning system.

Classification Tree Variable Importance

Most important variables in the full classification tree
variable importance
Absolute Magnitude H 731.0
Average Diameter (km) 730.4
Orbital Inclination 313.2
Orbital Eccentricity 234.7
Relative Velocity (km/s) 229.7
Miss Distance (km) 203.6
Orbital Semi-Major Axis 197.4

The model’s most important variables reinforce the visual findings: size and brightness are central to the hazardous label, while the other orbital variables provide additional but weaker separation.

Skepticism and Limitations

The strongest limitation is that the hazardous label is not an independent scientific outcome like a future impact. It is NASA’s classification, and that classification is partly based on variables related to asteroid size and brightness. NASA/JPL CNEOS defines PHAs using Earth MOID of 0.05 AU or less and absolute magnitude H of 22.0 or brighter (CNEOS NEO Groups). Absolute magnitude is therefore definition-adjacent, and average diameter is closely related to the same idea.

The dataset also does not include Earth MOID, the official orbital-distance criterion used in the PHA definition. We use miss_distance_km, but close-approach miss distance is not the same as MOID. This means the model approximates NASA’s classification using the available object-level variables rather than exactly applying NASA’s rule.

Other limitations:

  • The hazardous class is imbalanced, so accuracy can be misleading.
  • Rows with missing modeling fields were removed rather than imputed.
  • The close-approach variables appear to summarize one approach field, not the full future distribution of possible close approaches.
  • The models predict NASA’s label; they do not estimate causal asteroid danger or actual Earth impact probability.

Conclusion

The strongest pattern in the data is that NASA’s hazardous label is closely tied to asteroid size and brightness. Hazardous asteroids are rare in the cleaned dataset, making up about 6.6% of observations, and the full classification tree catches more hazardous objects than the skeptical tree. When size and brightness are removed, the model mostly defaults to predicting the majority non-hazardous class.

The main takeaway is therefore not that we built a danger-prediction system. Instead, we showed that NASA’s PHA label can be partly reproduced from available asteroid measurements, while also showing why that result must be interpreted carefully. The model is strongest when it includes variables that are close to NASA’s own definition.

Future work should add Earth MOID, the official orbital-distance part of the PHA definition, and should compare multiple close approaches for the same asteroid rather than relying on one summarized approach field. It would also be useful to separate asteroids by size group or orbit type to see whether the same patterns hold within more specific asteroid categories.
